title: Systemic Risk and Monetary Policy: The Haircut Gap Channel of the Lender of Last Resort
abstract: We show that lender of last resort (LOLR) policy exacerbates bank interconnectedness. Using novel micro-level data, we analyze LOLR’s haircut gaps: the differences between the private market and central bank haircuts. LOLR policy incentivizes banks to increase pledging and holdings of higher haircut-gap bonds, especially those issued by domestic and systemically important banks. Effects only apply to banks, not to nonbanks without LOLR access. LOLR funding revives bank bond issuance associated with higher haircut gaps and increases the subsequent correlation between pledging and issuing banks’ bond prices, in particular during periods of low-market returns and for domestic, systemically important banks.

author.Luc Laeven: European Central Bank,Germany, Tilburg University, the Netherlands, and CEPR, France
author.José-Luis Peydró: Imperial College London,UK, ICREA—Universitat Pompeu Fabra—BSE, Spain, and CEPR, France

In response to liquidity squeezes and market malfunctions during the Global Financial Crisis, central banks expanded their liquidity provision to ensure the proper functioning of the banking sector and the transmission of monetary policy. Such liquidity provision is intended to be targeted to temporary illiquidity, thereby absorbing systemic illiquidity risk. However, the line between illiquidity and insolvency gets blurred during a systemic crisis, giving rise to concerns that liquidity provision may be excessive. These large-scale operations have therefore renewed the debate on the constraints and boundaries of lender of last resort (LOLR) policies, including their implications for moral hazard and the exposure of the central bank to undue credit risk (e.g.,Bindseil et al. 2017;Calomiris, Flandreau, and Laeven 2016;Drechsler et al. 2016;Nyborg 2016). Crucially, as banks access liquidity facilities by posting securities as collateral, LOLR policies may affect banks’ interlinkages through common collateral exposures. Understanding the pledging and holdings of securities by banks is particularly important as financial interlinkages among banks can exacerbate systemic risk (e.g.,Acemoglu, Ozdaglar, and Tahbaz-Salehi 2015;Allen and Gale 2000;Rochet and Tirole 1996). Still, after more than a decade of experience with large-scale liquidity operations, little is known about whether and how LOLR policies may affect systemic risk.

In this paper, we show that LOLR policy affects bank interconnectedness and hence exacerbates the build-up of systemic risk in the banking sector. Our analysis uses a novel micro-level data set that links the securities held and pledged by banks to obtain LOLR funding with the haircuts applied by the LOLR and by private repo markets. We examine the LOLR’s haircut gap: the difference in valuation haircuts applied by the private market and the central bank for securities that can be pledged as collateral in repo operations. The haircut gap is an indication of the expected funding “subsidy” to banks on their borrowing from the central bank. Such subsidies are rationalized on the grounds of market failures (Freixas and Rochet 1998;Rochet and Vives 2004).

We find that LOLR funding provides incentives for banks to increase their pledging and holdings of bonds associated with higher haircut gaps. The pledging and holdings effects of haircut gap are stronger for bonds issued by other banks than by sovereigns. The resultant rise in bank interconnectedness is salient especially across domestic, systemically important banks. These results are consistent with theories of systemic bailout expectations in the event of a systemic crisis (e.g.,Acharya and Yorulmazer 2007;Farhi and Tirole 2012). Importantly, the effects on bond holdings are present only for banks, that have access to the LOLR, and not for nonbanks, that cannot borrow from the LOLR. Further, the pledging effects are more pronounced for banks with lower liquidity ratios and for banks with lower credit risk ratings, consistent with liquidity constraints and expected subsidy maximization motives for seeking central bank liquidity. We also document that LOLR policy stimulates bank bond issuance at lower spreads and longer maturities, consistent with an increase in the demand for bank bonds with higher haircut gaps. Finally, we find that an increase in holdings of other banks’ bonds triggered by higher haircut gaps subsequently increases the correlation of bond prices between pledging and issuing banks. The effects are stronger for systemically important banks and in times of market turbulence. Our results therefore uncover an important role of bank bonds as a source of collateral for other banks and show that LOLR policy increases systemic risk at the margin by increasing bank interconnectedness.

Our analysis focuses on the provision of liquidity by the European Central Bank (ECB). The focus on the euro area offers an ideal setting in which to study the implications of LOLR liquidity provision because the liquidity situation of the European banking sector was adversely affected by both the Global Financial Crisis and the European Sovereign Debt Crisis, and the ECB played an active role in providing liquidity to the banking sector. Total bank borrowing from the ECB more than doubled during the Global Financial Crisis and reached unprecedented levels (a peak of EUR 1.2 trillion) in the middle of the European Sovereign Debt Crisis.

We build a unique micro-level data set that provides a detailed picture of the ECB and private repo markets. First, we observe information on the banks’ security pledging with the ECB in LOLR operations. The data covers individual security (ISIN) level information on more than 20,000 bank- and government-issued securities pledged as collateral by all reporting counterparties to obtain LOLR funding; that is, the data are at the ISIN-bank-month level. In addition to the amount of liquidity obtained by each individual bank at any given date, we observe rich information on the corresponding collateral pledged, including issuer, haircut, rating, residual maturity, security type, amount pledged, and amount outstanding. Second, we link the confidential ECB data with private market haircuts provided by the two leading exchange and clearing houses in Europe: LCH Clearnet and EUREX. Our main data set is at the ISIN-bank-month level over the period from January 2009 to March 2015. We end the sample in March 2015 to avoid an overlap with the start of ECB quantitative easing that could potentially confound our findings. We complement our main data set with data on banks’ security holdings at the ISIN-country-quarter level.1

Our data set allows us to assess the importance of the haircut gap at the most granular level with extraordinary breadth and coverage. We construct a security-level time-varying haircut gap, that is, the difference between the private market and ECB valuation of each pledged security at a specific time. A high haircut gap means that the ECB applies a much more favorable valuation of the asset compared to the private repo market. Hence, it increases the borrowing capacity of banks against the collateral pledged with the ECB. Haircut gap measures the expected funding “subsidy” to banks on borrowing from the ECB. The highest haircut gaps are observed on bonds issued in periphery countries, especially in the height of the Sovereign Debt Crisis.

Our analysis explores the build-up of systemic risk arising from the holding of bank bonds by other interconnected banks. We focus on bank bonds for two main reasons. First, bank bond holdings are concentrated within the banking sector (e.g.,Bekaert and Breckenfelder 2019), while government debt is widely held across a range of sectors, such as pension funds and insurance companies (seeFigure 1). This is consistent with banking being a particularly opaque industry (e.g.,Morgan 2002). Second, while government bonds constitute an important source of pledged collateral, bank bonds represent the largest share of collateral type used in the ECB’s liquidity operations (seeFigure 2). Bank bonds tend to yield higher expected returns than government bonds and tend to be more liquid than bank loans. Moreover, being a marketable security, bank bonds tend to enjoy more favorable haircuts than bank loans when used in central bank operations. They therefore constitute a large fraction of bank’s asset holdings and pledged collateral. These considerations motivate our interest in exploring the role of bank bonds as collateral in LOLR operations, an area that has been largely overlooked by the literature.

We exploit the variation in the haircut gap across securities and over time and show that banks increase the pledging of securities featuring higher haircut gaps. The effect is stronger for securities issued by other banks compared to sovereign debt. As bank bonds are primarily held by other banks, that have access to LOLR funding, bank bonds are also more sensitive to haircut gaps than government bonds. In terms of magnitudes, a one-standard-deviation increase in the haircut gap (12 percentage points) is associated with a 1.9% increase in the pledging of bank-issued securities compared to its mean value at the bank level.

We also find that this effect is stronger for less liquid banks (as measured by the bank’s liquid assets ratio), consistent with liquidity constraints motive being an important driver of the pledging of bank bonds in accessing central bank liquidity. Moreover, when the central bank sets lower haircuts than the market for securities pledged, the expected subsidy maximization could be a key driver of pledging behavior with a view to extract higher expected funding “subsidies” by the central bank. Consistent with such a motive, we find that the haircut gap channel is stronger for banks with lower credit risk ratings (ie, for banks that are closer to their point of failure). The effect of one-standard-deviation higher haircut gap on pledging of bank bonds are by 10.9–12.5 pp stronger for one-standard-deviation less liquid (pledging) banks and by 5.5–7.4 pp stronger for one-standard-deviation lower-rated (pledging) banks. To corroborate the importance of the expected subsidy maximization, we also provide direct evidence of this motive by examining the relationship between the amount of security pledged and held for different levels of the haircut gap. We find that conditioning on the same amount of security held, banks pledge significantly more bonds with higher haircut gap. Specifically, banks pledge 90% of held securities that feature high haircut gap and 50% of low-haircut-gap securities held. This finding is consistent with banks actively maximizing expected haircut subsidy from securities held. In particular, banks are more likely to borrow from the central bank against the collateral offering the highest haircut gap.

By providing liquidity against collateral at different haircuts than the market, LOLR policies may change the security pledging and holdings by banks. Banks can use bank-to-bank linkages for risk diversification purposes (e.g.,Allen and Gale 2007), for instance, to obtain additional liquidity when bank payoffs are lower, or when lower bank asset fundamentals give rise to funding liquidity problems (e.g.,Rochet and Vives 2004). On the other hand, banks can use interbank asset linkages to increase correlation across banks and bank interconnectedness, for instance, by increasing exposure to each other, especially to more systemically important banks, thereby increasing the probability of systemic bailouts by the governments should banks fail (e.g.,Acharya and Yorulmazer 2007;Farhi and Tirole 2012;Freixas and Rochet 2008).

Consistent with theories of bailout expectations in the event of a systemic crisis, our results suggest that LOLR policy induces banks to pledge bonds with higher haircut gaps, especially issued by domestic banks and by systemically important banks. Banks increase the pledging of domestic bank bonds, especially in distressed periphery countries of Europe.2The effect of the haircut gap on the pledging of domestic bank bonds is around two times stronger than the average effect (3.5% increase compared to the mean value of pledged securities at the bank level). Within domestic banks, we find that higher haircut gaps increase the pledging of securities issued by systemically important banks, in line with the same theories of bailout expectations. A one-standard-deviation increase in the haircut gap is associated with a 5.2% increase in the pledging of domestic bank bonds issued by systemically important banks compared to the mean value of pledged securities. In the baseline analysis, we adopt SRISK (Brownlees and Engle 2017) as a measure of systemic risk, but results are robust to other measures, such as bank size.

We also show that changes in the pledging behavior of banks reflect actual changes in the holdings of securities and not merely the delayed pledging of previously held collateral. We do so by using data on the security holdings of banks reported at the ISIN-country-time level. Consistent with the pledging analysis, we show that banks increase the holdings of bank bonds with higher haircut gaps, especially if those bank bonds are domestic and from periphery countries. These effects are also stronger for bank bonds issued by systemically important banks. Therefore, these results support the hypothesis of higher bank interconnectedness associated to the LOLR haircut gap. Further, the holding results are consistent and quantitatively comparable with the pledging results.

We provide an external validity of our findings by testing whether the systemic risk effects of higher haircut gaps can be directly attributed to LOLR policy. To assess the role of LOLR, we exploit the effects of the haircut gap on holdings of bank securities across different sectors. We include both banks (that have access to LOLR liquidity) and other financial sectors, such as mutual funds, pension funds, and insurance companies (placebo sectors that do not have access to LOLR liquidity). We show that higher haircut gaps are associated with an increase in holding of bank-issued bonds only by other banks. On the contrary, the effect is negative for nonbanks that do not have access to LOLR. Moreover, the effects are disproportionately smaller for sovereign bonds, that are held across many sectors without ability to borrow from the central bank. Instead, bank bond holdings are largely concentrated within the banking system that has access to LOLR.

Next, we consider two identification strategies that rely on specific institutional features of the ECB liquidity policy to isolate the effect of haircut gaps on bank pledging. In the first identification strategy, we exploit the nonlinearities in the haircut gap with respect to rating. This identification relies on the fact that while the market haircut changes with every notch downgrade, the ECB only applies two levels of haircuts across the rating scale.3Hence, for a given security type, the ECB haircut profile displays only one jump, that is, from A- to BBB+. This institutional feature allows us to empirically test the hypothesis that following a one-notch downgrade, the pledging of domestic bonds with the ECB increases by more at ratings that trigger a larger jump in the haircut gap.

The second identification strategy exploits the fact that, contrary to the private markets, the ECB considers only the first best rating across the four rating agencies (S&P, Moody’s, Fitch, and DBRS). As a result, a one-notch downgrade at A- changes the ECB haircut valuation only if it affects the first best rating (binding downgrade). On the contrary, if the downgrade does not affect the first best rating, only the private market haircut will be increased, while the ECB haircut remains unchanged (nonbinding downgrade). Hence, this strategy allows to isolate the impact of changes in haircut gaps from changes in bond riskiness. While both nonbinding and binding downgrades increase the perceived riskiness of the security, they have opposite effect on the haircut gap: nonbinding downgrades increase the size of the haircut gap whereas binding downgrades decrease it. We test the hypothesis that nonbinding downgrades at A- that do not affect the first best rating provide incentives for banks to pledge more of the distressed asset with the LOLR than binding downgrades.

Our results are robust to exploiting these two ECB rules, as well as to a number of other validation tests. First, we show that the effect of haircut gaps on pledging of bank bonds issued by domestic and systemically important banks remains strong even when controlling for domestic sovereign debt pledging. Second, in addition to the baseline ISIN, bank, time and rating group fixed effects, we introduce pledging bank—issuing bank pair fixed effects as well as a more stringent version using pledging bank—issuing ISIN pair fixed effects that control for time-invariant financing relationship between banks. Third, we show that the results are not driven by banks’ common exposure to country risk, including sovereign risk, by introducing issuer country—time fixed effects. Fourth, we introduce time-varying issuer or security fixed effects to address a potential concern that higher haircut gaps are merely reflective of higher security riskiness, which would imply that the findings might be driven by risk-taking behavior by other banks. Relatedly, the results are robust to the introduction of bank-time fixed effects, which capture all unobserved time-varying pledging bank fundamentals.

Further, we show that banks whose debt is associated with higher haircut gaps respond to an increased demand for their bonds by issuing more securities. A one-standard-deviation increase in haircut gap is associated with a 7.5% to 10.7% increase in debt outstanding (issuance of bank bonds) at the bank level. Moreover, newly issued higher haircut gap bonds feature lower spreads and longer maturity, consistent with a higher demand for this type of bonds from other banks. This effect is particularly strong for banks in distressed periphery economies. We also examine the implications of the haircut gap channel of LOLR for the interconnectedness between bond issuing and pledging/holding banks through their balance sheet adjustments. From the perspective of issuing banks, importantly, we find that banks with higher haircut gaps do not merely use the market timing to replace their maturing debt but instead increase their overall dependence on bond market financing and do not substitute with other bank-to-bank linkages, such as the interbank loans.4Consistently, for issuing banks, the increase in financing through bank bond leads to higher total liabilities and higher leverage. From the perspective of bond pledging/holding banks, we first confirm an increase in the ECB liquidity and bank bond holdings on the balance sheet level. Second and more importantly, we do not find any effect on lending to other banks via the interbank market. As a result, the higher volume of bank bonds held among banks contributes to a stronger interconnectedness in the domestic banking sectors.

Next, we examine the impact of the haircut gap on market-based measures of systemic risk. Consistent with the main results on pledging, holdings and the systemic risk within the banking sector, we show that in response to the increased cross-holdings of bank bonds, banks become more correlated in market outcomes. We find that higher haircut gaps are associated with higher ex post correlation in bond prices between domestic pledging and issuing banks.5The effects are stronger for systemically important banks and in times of market turbulence. A one-standard-deviation higher haircut gap is associated with a 7.3% stronger correlation in bond prices between banks during periods of low market returns, and, moreover, the rise in correlation is the strongest (an increase by 14.1%) for domestic, systemically important issuing banks. Finally, we show that a high haircut gap is associated with a high correlation of bond prices especially in periods of high sovereign stress, that is consistent with the presence of “doom loop” style spiral effects. Our results suggest that the interbank holdings of bonds led to an accentuation of the doom loop in response to domestic sovereign stress. Taken together, these findings suggest that a rise in interconnectedness between banks triggered by LOLR policy plays out to the disadvantage of banks as it led to stronger comovement in market outcomes between banks, especially with larger drops in bond prices during bad times.

Our work contributes to several strands of literature. A large body of LOLR literature has provided theoretical arguments regarding the beneficial effects of LOLR policy arising from the provision of liquidity to temporarily illiquid but otherwise solvent institutions (e.g.,Bagehot 1873;Diamond and Dybvig 1983;Freixas, Rochet, and Parigi 2010;Rochet and Vives 2004;Stein 2012), with more recent evidence showing the positive effects on lending (e.g.,Cahn, Duquerroy, and Mullins 2018;Jasova, Mendicino, and Supera 2021;van Bekkum, Gabarro, and Irani 2018) and financial markets (e.g.,Pelizzon et al. 2020).

Our paper most closely relates toDrechsler et al. (2016), who show that ECB liquidity operations are accessed more by ex ante weakly capitalized banks that use the central bank funding to purchase more risky distressed sovereign bonds during the European Sovereign Debt Crisis. Another related paper isCrosignani, Faria-e Castro, and Fonseca (2020), who show that ECB’s 3-year LTRO liquidity provision had an unintended consequence of Portuguese banks loading up on domestic government bonds. A key difference between these two papers and our paper is that they analyze purchases of government debt, while our focus is on the pledging and holdings of bank bonds, an angle so far unexplored in the literature. More generally, we complement previous findings by presenting evidence on the effects of LOLR policy on bank interconnectedness (and hence the build-up of systemic risk in the banking sector). It is important to stress, however, that our results do not imply that LOLR increases overall systemic risk but that it increases systemic risk at the margin by encouraging bank interconnectedness via haircut gaps.

Our paper also relates to the literature on systemic risk. A large theoretical literature analyzes the systemic risk emerging from bank-to-bank linkages (e.g.,Acemoglu, Ozdaglar, and Tahbaz-Salehi 2015;Allen and Gale 2000;Cabrales, Gottardi, and Vega-Redondo 2017;Freixas, Parigi, and Rochet 2000;Rochet and Tirole 1996). While they focus on the interbank loan market as a source of systemic risk, we explore systemic risk that arises from bank interconnectedness via the cross-holding of bank bonds.6We add to this literature by showing that bank bonds dominate the securities pledged as a collateral with the central bank and contribute to an increase in bank interconnectedness. Through higher haircut gaps, the LOLR stimulates the build-up of systemic risk via banks increasing holdings and pledging of bonds issued by other domestic banks, especially in distressed periphery countries. Moreover, our results show that banks hold and pledge more bonds issued by domestic, systemically important banks. These results are consistent with systemic bailout theories (e.g.,Acharya and Yorulmazer 2007;Farhi and Tirole 2012;Freixas and Rochet 2008). Our findings are also closely related toGoldstein et al. (2020), who show that homogeneity in banks’ asset holdings amplifies the fragility of the financial sector. Our results also relate toBekaert and Breckenfelder (2019), who show that cross-holdings of bank bonds are large. Specifically, they use the inclusion of some banks to the list of other systemically important institutions to document the reallocation of bank risk in the euro area.

We also connect to the literature on the sovereign and bank risk nexus. While the implications of LOLR policy for the sovereign-bank nexus are well understood (e.g.,Acharya and Steffen 2015;Altavilla, Pagano, and Simonelli 2017;Battistini, Pagano, and Simonelli 2014;Drechsler et al. 2016), the impact of LOLR on the interconnectedness and concentration of bank risks has so far been unexplored. We show that bank bonds are an important source of collateral (in addition to government bonds) and exhibit higher sensitivity to LOLR policy compared to government bonds.

Finally, this paper also links to the literature on monetary policy and risk-taking (e.g.,Adrian and Shin 2010;Allen and Rogoff 2011;Dell’Ariccia, Laeven, and Suarez 2017;Di Maggio and Kacperczyk 2017;Jimenez et al. 2014). Our paper provides evidence on systemic bank risk-taking through LOLR facilities. In addition, by exploring the systemic risk implications of central bank liquidity policy in crisis times we complement the growing number of studies that assess the implications of unconventional policies for financial markets (e.g.,Chodorow-Reich 2014;Koijen et al. 2021;Krishnamurthy, Nagel, and Vissing-Jorgensen 2017;Krishnamurthy and Vissing-Jorgensen 2011) and bank lending (e.g.,Chakraborty, Goldstein, and MacKinlay 2018;Di Maggio, Kermani, and Palmer 2020;Heider, Saidi, and Schepens 2019;Peydro, Polo, and Sette 2021;Rodnyansky and Darmouni 2017).
This section presents stylized facts regarding the key holders of bank and government bonds in the euro area. In addition, it provides relevant information on the ECB liquidity framework and the pledging behavior by banks.

1.1 Bank bond holdings

Figure 1compares the holdings of bank and government securities in the euro area, that are at the core of our analysis. European banks hold a remarkably large share of bonds issued by (other) banks. Further, while banks are the largest holders of bank-issued bonds in the economy, the holding of government bonds is more equally spread across different institutional sectors. In addition, banks generally hold largest shares of domestically issued securities, including bank bonds. Hence, bank risk is heavily concentrated in the domestic banking sector. This is consistent with banking being a particularly opaque industry (e.g.,Morgan 2002).

1.2 Central bank liquidity operations

The ECB provides liquidity in the form of repurchase agreements (repo) to banks operating in the euro area.7In the aftermath of the Global Financial Crisis, the ECB strengthened the LOLR function embedded in the regular monetary policy operations and “adopted a genuinely classical approach to […] LOLR responsibilities” (Praet 2016).8

Total bank borrowing from the ECB increased remarkably following the Lehman Brothers collapse and reached a peak of EUR 1.2 trillion in the middle of the European Sovereign Debt Crisis. The eurozone, hence, represents an ideal laboratory to study LOLR policy of unprecedented magnitude.9

1.2.1 Eligible collateral

To ensure the availability of sufficient collateral across a large number of banks and countries, the ECB provides liquidity against a wide range of eligible assets. Securities eligible as a collateral for LOLR operations need to meet the minimum requirement of a first best credit assessment of at least credit quality step 3 (rating of BBB- or equivalent) obtained from external credit assessment institutions (S&P, Moody’s, Fitch and DBRS).10

1.2.2 Pledged collateral

What do banks pledge with the LOLR as a collateral?Figure 2, panel A, shows that while sovereign debt (in blue) constitutes an important source of pledged collateral, bank-issued bonds (in red) represent the largest share of collateral asset type pledged with the ECB.Figure 2, panel B, zooms-in on the pledging of bank-issued securities and compares the pledging of domestic and foreign bank-issued securities by geographical location of the pledging bank (core versus periphery).11Domestic bank-issued bonds were more used as a collateral by banks located in core countries in the aftermath of the Global Financial Crisis. However, since the start of the European Sovereign Debt Crisis, banks located in distressed periphery countries remarkably increased their pledging of bonds issued by other domestic banks.

Changes in the pledging behavior broadly reflect the actual changes in the security holding portfolios of banks.Internet Appendix Figure B4shows a distinct increase in the holdings of domestic bank bonds by other banks in periphery countries in periods of high haircut gaps, a pattern that is consistent with the evolution of pledging of bank issued bonds (Figure 2, panel B). The timing of the pledging of newly issued securities suggests that banks pledge over 75% of newly acquired securities within three months from the security issuance date (Figure 3). This is also consistent with the previous micro-data evidence that documents that security holding and pledging of banks are strongly correlated (seeJasova, Mendicino, and Supera 2021).

1.2.3 Haircuts

The amount of liquidity a bank can obtain from the ECB against the collateral pledged crucially depends on the applied haircut. The ECB haircut valuation is primarily determined by a combination of issuer group (government, bank, etc), asset type (covered bonds, uncovered bonds, etc), coupon type (floating and fixed), rating and residual maturity. The basic valuation matrix as well as any temporary or permanent changes to this valuation framework are publicly communicated and made available by the ECB.12Additionally, the ECB publishes daily the list of eligible securities (at the ISIN level) that allows us to precisely observe the haircut applied to each security submitted as a collateral in LOLR operations.13

Prior to the Global Financial Crisis, the haircuts applied by the ECB were similar to the private market haircuts on repo loans. However, afterward, the ECB started offering haircuts significantly below that of the private repo markets. Specifically, over the 2009–2015 period, the ECB applied average haircuts of 5.9%, while private market average haircuts were close to 12% (seeTable 1).

While the central bank offers below-market (“subsidized”) haircuts, it charges an interest rate above the private repo rates (Drechsler et al. 2016). In line with the Bagehot principle, the ECB provides unlimited allotment of funds to European banks against adequate collateral at a “penalty” rate. Hence, for the collateral associated with very low haircut gaps, banks have a higher incentive to borrow in private markets.14
article.2 Data:
We construct a unique micro-level data set that matches ECB data and links the securities held and pledged by banks to obtain LOLR funding with haircuts applied by the LOLR and by private repo markets, and bank balance sheet data. The richness and granularity of our data set allows us to exploit the impact of LOLR policy via the haircut gap channel across a large number of securities, banks and countries. The data set covers the January 2009 to March 2015 period, hence, capturing the European Sovereign Debt Crisis.15To the best of our knowledge, our paper is the first to provide a systematic and comprehensive assessment of the haircut gap channel of LOLR policy.

2.1 Central bank liquidity and pledged collateral

The Market Operations Database (MOPDB) is an ECB internal source that contains granular data on all liquidity operations and collateral pledged by European banks to obtain liquidity from the ECB. This is the largest cross-country liquidity and collateral data set covering the monetary policy operations of the euro area. In addition to the amount of liquidity obtained by each individual bank at any given date, MOPDB provides detailed information for more than 20,000 unique bank- and government-issued securities pledged as collateral by banks. The main variables used in the analysis are International Security Identification Number (ISIN), issuer group (bank, government, etc), security type (bond, note, covered bond, ABS, etc), issuance and maturity date. MOPDB also reports the amount pledged by each individual bank and the total amount outstanding. In addition, it reports information on valuation (market value, ECB haircut and haircut-adjusted value). We use this information at the level of each ISIN-bank-time (where time is monthly level data). This is crucial for our analysis, as it allows to assess the importance of LOLR policy at the most granular level and with extraordinary breadth and coverage.

2.2 Private haircuts

Next, we create a novel data set that provides a detailed picture of the ECB and private repo markets in Europe. To this end, we collect private repo market data from the two leading exchange and clearing houses in Europe: LCH Clearnet and EUREX. Our analysis uses the market haircuts of securities posted in the private markets in two ways. We either analyze all possible securities (imputing the private haircut for some securities via a random forest machine learning prediction algorithm) or analyze only the securities with private haircuts (raw sample) from the clearing houses. Our results are robust to both samples.16

2.3 Security holdings data

We use Securities Holdings Statistics (SHS) data that obtain information on ISIN-level holdings of debt securities. The data are reported at the ISIN-sector-country-quarter level separately for all institutional sectors in the euro area.17We use the holding data in three ways. First, we focus on the bond holdings within the banking sector only to provide evidence of the rise of bank interconnectedness in bond holding portfolios in response to high haircut gaps. Second, we use the entire richness of the data set to compare the evolution of bond holdings by banks (the sector with access to the LOLR funding) versus nonbank financial institutions (sectors without access to the LOLR funding, such as investment firms, pension, and mutual funds) to present additional validity of the LOLR’s haircut gap channel. Third, we replicate the main results on pledging for the holdings data and compare the results obtained using pledging data with those using holdings data.

2.4 Securities and issuer ratings

The Centralized Securities Database (CSDB) consolidates security-by-security data from both ECB internal and commercial sources. It provides ratings on the credit quality of marketable securities according to the external credit assessment institutions: S&P, Moody’s, Fitch, and DBRS. The information is reported at the ISIN-time and issuer-time level for all securities issued by EU residents or denominated in euro.

2.5 Bank-level data

Finally, we link the collateral and liquidity data to bank-level data. The proprietary Individual Balance Sheet Items (IBSI) database, maintained by the ECB, contains monthly-level information on the granular asset and liability categories for about 340 most important banks operating in the euro area. We construct time-varying bank level variables, such as size, borrowing, and lending, in the interbank market, deposits, borrowing through bonds, bond holdings, liquidity ratio, and equity ratio. Furthermore, we collect data on bank ownership structure from Bankscope and the Register of Institutions and Affiliates Database (RIAD) to create the group structure of banks. Using this information, we can exclude the pledging of bank bonds issued by the same bank or by the same bank holding group. As a result, in the analysis we only examine the pledging of bank bonds that were issued byotherbanks. Finally, we use information on banks’ bond issuances, maturity and prices (yield-to-maturity), equity prices and leverage from Datastream to (a) construct the SRISK measure (seeBrownlees and Engle 2017),18(b) construct a measure of bond price correlation between pairs of pledging and issuing banks, and (c) analyze the impact of haircut gap on bond issuances.
article.3 Empirical Strategy:
3.1 Haircut gap

The haircut gap is the difference between the private market and ECB valuation of a pledged security s at time t : HaircutGap s , t = Private market haircut s , t − ECB haircut s , t . (1)

In the baseline specification, the private market haircut for securitysin monthtis imputed with a machine learning random forest technique that uses security’s observable characteristics (issuer group, issuer rating, coupon type and residual maturity basket).19In the robustness, we show that our findings hold also when using only the raw (unimputed) data. The ECB haircuts are directly observed in the ECB’s Market Operation Database at the ISIN-month level.20

Table 1provides the summary statistics. The average haircut gap for securities in our sample is 6.1 percentage points with a standard deviation of 12 percentage points. The ECB applied average haircut of 5.9%, while private market haircuts averaged to 12%.Figure 4illustrates the diverging trajectory of the haircut gap by securities issued in core and periphery countries. Since the downgrade of Greece in April 2010, the eurozone experienced a wave of rating downgrades of securities in periphery countries. These rating downgrades substantially increased private market haircuts and thus limited the borrowing capacity of banks from private repo market against this collateral. In contrast, the ECB did not increase the haircuts on securities to the extent observed in the private market. Instead, the LOLR haircut valuation was based on the change in ratings as explained in Section 1.

The deepening of the European Sovereign Debt Crisis therefore exacerbated the gap between the private and ECB haircuts on the securities issued in periphery countries.Figure 4illustrates a significant increase in the haircut gap for securities issued in periphery countries. We also document that this overall divergence holds for periphery countries at large even if we exclude the unique case of Greek bonds (seeInternet Appendix).21

3.2 Full sample analysis

We exploit the variation in the haircut gap at the cross-section of securities (ISIN level) and over time to examine the effects of changes in the haircut gap on the pledging and holding behavior of banks in the euro area using the full sample of securities. We test the hypothesis that banks increase pledging and holding of securities characterized by higher haircut gaps. We also investigate the heterogeneous effects across different securities and banks. The baseline empirical specification estimates how changes in the haircut gap affect bank security pledging and holding with the LOLR. Equation (2) summarizes the setup: α α α α β β γ δ ϵ y s , z , t = α t + α s + α r + α z + β 1 HaircutGap s , t − 1 + β 2 ( HaircutGap s , t − 1 × H s , z ) + γ H s , z + δ X s , t − 1 + ϵ s , z , t , (2) where z ∈ ( b , c ) denotes either a pledging bank b or a country of a holding bank c . Specifically, in the pledging analysis, the main outcome variable denotes the pledged value of security s by bank b in month t , expressed as a share of the total value outstanding of this security. This allows us to examine the importance of the pledging with LOLR over time rescaled by the total amount available. 22 Similarly, in the holding analysis, the outcome variable is the held value of security s by the banking sector of country c in quarter t , expressed as a share of the total value outstanding of this security. 23 We start by examining the effect of haircut gaps on marketable securities issued by both the governments and banks in euro area countries. In the main analysis of the paper, which examines the systemic risk arising from bank interconnectedness, we explicitly focus on bank-issued securities.

HaircutGaps,t−1refers to the difference in the haircut valuation of securitysby private repo markets and the central bank, as described inEquation (1). In addition to the average effect of the haircut gap on the banks’ pledging behavior, we also focus on the heterogeneous effects. InEquation (2), we refer to the heterogeneity in a general way asHs,z⁠. In Sections 4.1 to 4.4, we provide detailed explanations of these heterogeneities as we address different effects of the LOLR policy to explore various underlying channels.

Importantly, we saturate the specification with control variables (observables) and fixed effects. We introduce time fixed effects (αt) that capture time-varying common, unobserved developments in macro, financial and monetary conditions. Further, we saturate the specification with security (ISIN) fixed effects (αs). This allows us to control for permanent security-level characteristics (such as issuer, coupon payments, or the existence of guarantees). In addition, we progressively introduce time-varying controls (security price and residual maturity) denoted asXs,t−1⁠. We also use rating group fixed effects (αr) to control for security riskiness.24Finally, we use pledging bank fixed effects or holding banks’ country fixed effects in pledging and holding analysis, respectively. In Section 4.5, we corroborate the key findings by saturating the specification with even higher order fixed effects. Specifically, we add pledging bank—issuing bank pair or pledging bank—ISIN fixed effects to account for time-invariant financing relationship between banks. We further introduce issuing country—time and issuing bank—time fixed effects to control for sovereign and issuer risk. Finally, we also saturate the model with ISIN—time fixed effects to control for time-varying observed and unobserved security characteristics including its riskiness, and pledging bank—time fixed effects to absorb time-varying variation of the pledging bank (including, but not limited to, the time-varying demand for LOLR funding). Our standard errors are two-way clustered at the security and time levels.

3.3 ECB institutional rules

In the second part of the analysis, we restrict the sample to exploit two specific institutional features of the ECB haircut policy:(1)the two-step haircut profile and(2)the first best rating rule.

3.3.1 Kinks and jumps in haircut gaps

In this first identification strategy, we exploit the nonlinearities in the haircut gap sensitivity with respect to the rating. For each type of security the ECB applies only two discrete levels of haircuts: a lower haircut value if the rating is equal or better than A-, and a higher haircut if the rating ranges between BBB+ and BBB- notch.25Hence, while private markets adjust their haircut valuation at every notch downgrade, the ECB haircuts only react to rating changes between A- and BBB+. As a result, a (one-notch) change in ratings affects the haircut gap differently depending on the ex ante rating level.

Figure 5illustrates the intuition behind this identification strategy. The figure shows the relationship between the rating and the haircut levels for a representative class of pledged assets (ie, unsecured bond issued by credit institution with 1–3 years residual maturity and fixed coupon payments). Panel A depicts the haircuts (in percent) set by the ECB and the average haircuts applied by the private market. As mentioned, the ECB haircut profile (in red) is markedly flat with only one jump from A- to BBB+. That is, within a security class, the ECB haircut jumps only once (from A- to BBB+), and there are no other jumps between AAA and A- (low risk) or between BBB+ and BBB- (high risk). The private market haircut profile is instead upward sloping with substantial nonlinearities. Most notably, the private market on average applies large increments in haircuts at the bottom tier of the investment-grade rating.

Figure 5, panel B, shows the average haircut gap at each best rating notch. The differences in sensitivity to rating of haircuts applied by the ECB and the private market create a nonlinearity in the haircut gap profile with kink and jumps. For example, a one-notch downgrade from A+ to A yields on average an increase in haircut gap by 1 percentage point, while a downgrade from A to A- delivers a haircut gap hike of 6 percentage points.

To implement this strategy, we restrict the sample to the period of+/−2months around the event of a one-notch rating change and exploit the effect of the kinks and jumps in the nonlinear haircut gap profile on the banks’ pledging behavior described byEquation (2). We empirically test the hypothesis that following a one-notch downgrade, the security pledging with the ECB increases by more at rating notches, thereby triggering a larger jump in the haircut gap. Analogously, we use the kinks and jumps empirical strategy also in the holding analysis. Because of less granular data, we define the event window as+/−2quarters around the one-notch rating change.

3.3.2 Binding and nonbinding downgrades

The second identification strategy exploits another key institutional feature of the ECB haircut policy:the first best rating rule. While private markets generally react to the downgrade of any rating agency, the ECB considers only the best rating among the four rating agencies (S&P, Moody’s, Fitch and DBRS). That is, if a security is rated by the four rating agencies, the ECB only moves the haircut after the 4th downgrade, that is, after all the other three rating agencies already downgraded the security.

We restrict the sample of securities with the best rating at the A- cliff and we exploit the variation in the haircut gap following a one-notch downgrade. We distinguish between two types of downgrades from the perspective of the ECB:binding, ie, the best rating of the four agencies moves to BBB+, ornonbinding, that is, at least one rating agency continues to rate the debt at A- (ie, some, but not all, rating agencies have downgraded the security). In case of a binding downgrade, both ECB and private markets increase haircut levels. If the downgrade is nonbinding, ECB continues to apply the lower haircuts (associated with A-) and only private markets increase the haircuts. As a result, nonbinding downgrades increase the size of the haircut gap, while binding downgrades tend to decrease it.

This strategy allows us to address the empirical challenge that high haircut gaps can be driven by the high security risk (that is, that security risk and haircut gaps are correlated). While both nonbinding and binding downgrades increase the perceived riskiness of the security, they have opposite effect on the haircut gap: nonbinding downgrades increase the size of the haircut gap whereas binding downgrades decrease it. We test the hypothesis that nonbinding downgrades at A- that do not affect the first best rating provide incentives for banks to pledge more of the distressed asset with the LOLR than binding downgrades. An alternative approach to tackle this issue is to directly control for the issuer risk with issuer-time or security-time fixed effects. We present the results of this analysis using the full sample in Section 4.5.
article.4 Bank Pledging Behavior:
4.1 Pledging of government versus bank bonds

We start our analysis by examining the impact of the haircut gap on the security pledging behavior of banks to obtain LOLR funding. Motivated by the evidence in Section 1, we investigate the similarities and differences related to the use of bank- and government-issued bonds as a collateral in LOLR liquidity-providing repo operations.

The existing literature has largely focused on examining the role of government bonds (e.g.,Acharya and Steffen 2015;Crosignani, Faria-e Castro, and Fonseca 2020;Drechsler et al. 2016). How- ever, as shown inFigure 1, banks’ exposure to risk within the banking sector is at least as important as the exposure to sovereign risk. In addition, bank-issued debt is disproportionately more used as collateral with the ECB compared to government-issued debt and the importance of bank bonds pledged by banks located in periphery countries increased substantially during the Sovereign Debt Crisis. Hence, exploring the impact of LOLR on the pledging of bank-issued securities is crucial to understand its effect on bank interconnected and systemic risk.

Specifically, we first test the hypothesis that banks increase the pledging of securities featuring higher haircut gaps. Second, we explore the heterogeneous impact of the haircut gap on security pledging and examine the differential effects for bank bonds. The results further highlight that the impact of higher haircut gaps on banks’ pledging with the LOLR is stronger for bank bonds than for sovereign debt. In terms of economic effects, a one-standard-deviation increase in the haircut gap is associated with a 0.08 (0.05) percentage point increase in the pledging of bank (government) bonds as a share of bonds outstanding at the individual bank level. This effect is economically significant and corresponds to a 1.9% (1.2%) increase compared to the mean value of pledged bonds as a share of the value outstanding at the individual bank level (seeTable 1for the summary statistics).26As shown inFigure 1, the holdings of bank bonds are heavily concentrated in the balance sheets of banking institutions who have access to the LOLR facilities. These are, therefore, the type of securities more sensitive to changes in the haircut gap. Government bonds are instead more widely held by other financial institutions, such as pension funds or mutual funds. Consistently, these assets are less sensitive to changes in the valuation of the LOLR for the provision of liquidity, as the latter can be only exploited by banks.

To summarize, our results highlight that through favorable haircut gaps, the LOLR benefits more the pledging of bank-issued securities, as compared to government bonds. In Section 4.2, we explore how LOLR affects the pledging of different types of bank-issued bonds and their implications for the build-up of systemic risk in the banking sector.

4.2 Pledging of bank bonds: full sample

In this section and throughout the rest of the paper, we focus the analysis on bank bonds. We analyze what type of bank-issued securities benefited the most from a favorable haircut gap. This examination allows us to further test whether higher haircut gaps increase systemic risk.

We investigate whether the haircut gap provides incentives for banks to pledge bonds issued by (other) banks that are more similar to them (domestic versus foreign) or by systemically important banks (based on the SRISK measure). Importantly, we exclude the pledging of own issued and retained bonds, as well as bonds issued by other banks in the same banking group.

4.2.1 Domestic versus foreign bank bonds

We start by exploring variation within bank bonds to assess whether the haircut gap provides incentives to banks to increase their pledging of bonds issued by banks similar to them. To this end, we split pledged securities in two categories: domestic and foreign. A security is considered to be domestic if the pledging and issuing banks have the same country of location, and as foreign otherwise. Equation (4) summarizes the setup: α α α α β β γ δ ϵ Value pledged s , b , t Value outstanding s = α t + α s + α r + α b + + β 1 ( HaircutGap s , t − 1 × Domestic s , b ) + β 2 ( HaircutGap s , t − 1 × Foreign s , b ) + + γ Domestic s , b + δ X s , t − 1 + ϵ s , b , t . (4)

Column 4 ofTable 2presents the results. The increased pledging of bank-issued bonds associated with higher haircut gaps is attributable to the pledging of bonds issued by domestic banks. Foreign bank bonds instead deliver negative nonsignificant estimates. The effects of the haircut gap on the pledging of domestic bank bonds are around two times stronger than the average effect, that is, 3.5% increase compared to the mean value of pledged securities at the individual bank level.

4.2.2 Domestic bank bonds in core versus periphery

In light of this finding, we examine the following questions: Do we observe an increase in pledging of bank bonds within the banking sector across the eurozone as a whole? Or, is there a disproportionately larger effect in periphery countries that suffered from the Sovereign Debt Crisis and where security haircut gaps are the most sizable?

To address these questions, we complement the previous analysis with an additional source of regional heterogeneity. Motivated by the heterogeneous evolution of haircut gaps in eurozone’s core versus periphery ( Figure 4 ), we further split the pledging banks into two categories: Periphery b and Core b . The result in column 5 ofTable 2shows that the effects are driven by domestic banks located in periphery countries, where the haircut gap is the most pronounced and the risks in the banking sector are the highest.

4.2.3 Systemically important banks

We further explore the implications of the haircut gap for systemic risk through the role of systemically important banks. To this end, we use SRISK as a measure of the systemic risk contribution of banks. In line inBrownlees and Engle (2017), we compute SRISK as a function of bank’s size, leverage, and long-run marginal expected shortfall for all available euro area banks.27Finally, we construct a binary indicator that denotes banks as systemically important if their SRISK ratio is in the top 10% of SRISK of all banks in the respective country, and as zero otherwise.28

Given the results presented in previous section, we investigate the effects on systemically important banks also in interaction with the Domestic categorical variable. Column 6 ofTable 2reports the results. For the statistically significant effects, we find that, in response to higher haircut gaps banks pledge more securities issued by other domestic banks (⁠β3^=0.00899⁠). Importantly, we find a stronger response for securities issued by domestic, systemically important banks (⁠β1^=0.0224⁠). Interestingly, the increase in the pledging of bonds issued by banks with higher SRISK is not a generalized phenomenon, that is, the estimate ofβ2is negative (-0.0101), indicating that the stronger effect for systemically important banks is observed only within the domestic banking sector. The total additional effect for domestic, systemically important banks is the sum of two coefficients (⁠β1^+β2^⁠). We find thatβ1^+β2^is positive (0.0123) and statistically significant at 1% level. Taken together, this confirms that banks respond the most to higher haircuts of securities issued by domestic, systemically important banks.

In terms of economic effects, a one-standard-deviation increase in the haircut gap is associated with a 5.2% increase in the pledging of domestic bank bonds issued by systemically important banks compared to the mean value of pledged securities.29

Our findings on the pledging of domestic banks bonds and on domestic bank bonds issued by systemically important banks are consistent with the systemic bailout theory proposed byAcharya and Yorulmazer (2007)andFarhi and Tirole (2012). Higher haircut gaps on risky bonds issued by systemically important banks (which are “too big to fail”) in periphery countries (which suffered the crisis) incentivize the herding behavior by local banks and exacerbate the “too many to fail” problem.

4.3 Pledging of bank bonds: ECB institutional rules

A key insight from the full sample analysis is that LOLR policy provides incentives for banks to pledge bonds with higher haircut gaps issued by domestic, systemically important banks. In this section, we examine the effect of haircut gaps on systemic risk using two respective identification strategies introduced in Section 3.3. We revisit the estimation results from Section 4.2 by focusing on the two targeted identification strategies using ECB institutional rules.

Table 3is organized as follows. Columns 1–3 exploit the first identification strategy that uses nonlinear “kinks and jumps” in the haircut gap with respect to the rating. This identification relies on the fact that the market haircuts react to every notch downgrade, with larger increases in haircuts for downgrades at the bottom tier of the investment-grade rating. Instead, for the ECB, within a security class, the haircut only jumps once from A- to BBB+. There are no other jumps between AAA and A- (low risk) or between BBB+ and BBB- (high risk). This institutional feature allows us to empirically test the hypothesis that following a one-notch downgrade, the pledging of domestic bonds with the ECB increases by more at notches characterized by a larger jump in the haircut gap. Columns 4–6 report estimates of the second identification approach. Here, we narrow the sample to the securities at the A- rating cliff and we exploit the variation in the haircut gap following a one-notch downgrade. We get the identification from exploiting the fact that the ECB moves to the next BBB+ haircut notch only if the downgrade affects the first best rating, while the private market tends to react to any downgrade. As a result a one-notch downgrade from the A- is associated with a higher haircut gap if it does not move ECB valuation (the rating is nonbinding for the ECB).

4.4 Liquidity constraints and expected subsidy maximization

We further expand our analysis to understand the channels behind increased pledging of high haircut gap securities to access LOLR liquidity. Specifically, we examine the importance of liquidity versus expected subsidy maximization motives in driving our results.

On the one hand, when haircut gaps are higher, liquidity constrained banks could tap more liquidity from LOLR compared to the private repo market. If a liquidity motive is present, banks with worse liquidity position are expected to pledge more bonds with higher haircut gaps. On the other hand, the haircut gap is an indication of the expected funding “subsidy” to banks on their borrowing from the central bank as compared to private markets. Such subsidies are rationalized on the grounds of market failures (see e.g.,Freixas and Rochet 2008). As higher haircut gaps are associated with riskier securities, a risk-taking motive could be a key driver of pledging behavior with the aim of extracting higher expected “subsidies” from the LOLR, in the same vein as the risk-taking channel of monetary policy (e.g.,Becker and Ivashina 2015;Dell’Ariccia, Laeven, and Suarez 2017). If a subsidy maximization motive is driving bank behavior, we would expect that the haircut gap channel should be stronger for riskier banks that are closer to default.

We proxy for the liquidity constraint motive with bank liquidity ratio. Specifically, we use bank balance sheet data to compute the holdings of liquid assets (cash, reserves and securities) as a share of to total assets (cfKashyap and Stein (2000)for a similar measure). We proxy for the expected subsidy maximization motive by using the rating as a measure of bank riskiness (higher value implies worse rating) (cfMorgan (2002)), as subsidy maximization should play a more important role for worse rated (riskier) banks.

Table 4presents the results. Columns 1 and 2 rely on the baseline specification as described inEquation (2), where the heterogeneity denotes de-meaned liquidity ratio in column 1 and de-meaned bank rating in column 2.30The estimates show that our main result on increased pledging of bank bonds with higher haircut gap is stronger for liquidity constrained banks (proxied by lower liquid assets) and for worse rated banks. Column 3 horse-races the channels by including both interaction terms in the same regression simultaneously. We find that both motives are important in explaining our results. Columns 4–6 confirm the robustness of these results even after including the most stringent set of fixed effects from other robustness exercise, namely, pledging bank-time and ISIN-time fixed effects.31In terms of magnitudes, the effects of one-standard-deviation higher haircut gap on pledging of bank bonds are by 10.9- to 12.5-pp stronger for one-standard-deviation less liquid pledging banks and by 5.5- to 7.4-pp stronger for one-standard-deviation lower-rated pledging banks.

As we want to examine just the pure correlation between amount held and pledged for different levels of haircut gap, we do not include any fixed effects or controls in this regression.

We find that conditioning on the same amount of security held, banks pledge significantly more securities with higher haircut gap. Regarding the magnitudes, banks pledge 90% of held securities that feature high haircut gap and 50% of low haircut gap securities held. This finding is consistent with banks actively maximizing expected haircut subsidy from securities held. Specifically, banks are more likely to borrow from the central bank against the collateral offering the highest haircut gap/subsidy.

4.5 Additional results and robustness

In this section, we present a battery of robustness tests in support of the haircut gap channel of LOLR. Specifically, we show that our results are robust to controlling for sovereign and issuer risk, to the choice of dependent variable, to the sample construction, to the proxy for systemically important banks and to the measure of LOLR intervention.

4.5.1 Role of sovereign risk, issuer risk, and bank matching

In panel A, we report the results for the domestic versus foreign margin, and in panel B, we present evidence for the role of systematically important domestic banks.

To this end, we include an additional control variable that captures the ratio of bankbpledging of the domestic government bonds to their total amount outstanding. The coefficient estimates are virtually unchanged compared to the baseline results reported inTable 2, suggesting that the sensitivity of bank bond pledging to haircut gap changes is unaffected by concurrent sovereign bond pledging.

Next, we present results using pledging bank—issuing bank pair fixed effects as well as a more stringent version using pledging bank—ISIN pair fixed effects that control for time-invariant financing relationship between banks.Table 5, columns 2 and 3, reports the results, which are consistent with the rest of our estimates. We further revisit our baseline results and show that they are not driven by banks’ common exposure to country risk, including sovereign risk. To this end, we expand our baseline regressions with issuer country—time fixed effects to control for sovereign risk. The estimates presented in column 4 ofTable 5confirm that our original findings are robust. confirm that our original findings are robust.

Further, we test whether the systemic risk effects of the haircut gap are robust to controlling for unobserved time-varying riskiness, liquidity or overall issuance of securities by the same issuer in each time period. For this purpose, we saturate our baseline regressions with issuing bank—time fixed effects (column 5 ofTable 5) and even more granular ISIN—time fixed effects (column 6 ofTable 5).32These specifications also allow us to address the potential concern that a higher haircut gap could just reflect a higher issuer/security riskiness and, hence our results would merely capture risk-taking behavior by other banks. Our findings on the increased pledging effects of higher haircut gap domestic bank bonds are robust when controlling for time-varying issuer or security unobserved heterogeneity including its riskiness.

Finally, column 7 adds pledging bank—time fixed effects to the most stringent specification with ISIN—time FE reported in the previous column 6. Pledging bank—time fixed effects absorb any changes in behavior of a bank that pledges bonds to the LOLR (including, but not limited to, the time-varying changes in the pledging bank’s bond portfolio, changes in bank’s liquidity needs and demand for LOLR funding). Our findings remain robust.

4.5.2 Private market haircuts

Second, our findings are robust to different data methodologies regarding the imputation of the private market haircuts. In our baseline analysis, we use private market haircuts imputed with the random forest technique.33We show that the results are also robust to the use of Bayesian Model Averaging (BMA) or simple linear regressions.34The rationale behind the use of imputed data is related to the potential concern that high haircut gap securities may be underrepresented in the private market haircut data because banks with access to the LOLR facilities can obtain larger liquidity against them from the central bank. These securities still appear in our data set because they are used in the private market repo transactions by other financial institutions without access to the LOLR. Nevertheless, our results are also robust to the use of raw private market haircut data.35

4.5.3 Systemically important banks

Third, in addition to the complex measure of SRISK, we also repeat the analysis using the bank size as a simple proxy for the banks’ systemic importance. Consistently with the SRISK results, we find economically and statistically strong effects on the pledging of bank securities issued by domestic large banks.36

4.5.4 Binding and nonbinding downgrades

Fourth, we revisit the identification strategy on binding and nonbinding downgrades using ratings instead of the haircuts.37As shown in Section 3.3, nonbinding downgrades increase the size of the haircut gap, while binding downgrades decrease it. In this robustness analysis, we use a difference-in-difference research design and we define nonbinding downgrades asTreatedand binding downgrades asControl.38Consistent with our baseline results, we show that nonbinding downgrades (treatment group) provide more incentives for banks to increase their pledging with the LOLR as opposed to binding downgrades. This robustness exercise presents an alternative way to validate the effects of the LOLR on banks’ pledging without the direct use of haircuts.

4.5.5 vLTRO liquidity

Fifth, we investigate the effect of haircut gaps on bank bond pledging in the period of very long-term refinancing operations (vLTRO).39We find that the effects of haircut gap on the pledging of domestic bank bonds are even stronger in times of more accommodative LOLR stance (vLTRO period). We report these results and additional details of the vLTRO analysis in theInternet Appendix.

In this section, we show that changes in the pledging behavior of banks reflect actual changes in the holdings of securities, and not merely the pledging of previously held collateral. The joint analysis of holding and pledging of securities allows us to track the impact of LOLR policy on systemic risk in the banking sector. To this end, we use data on security holdings of banks available at the ISIN-country-quarter level and estimate the baselineEquation (2), where the outcome variable is the value held of security (ISIN)sby banks in countrycin quartert.

Taken together, we find that by providing a favorable haircut gap, LOLR contributes to an increase in the concentration of bank risk within the domestic banking sector, especially in periphery countries, where the risks were already the highest. Our findings provide new insights to explain the disproportional holdings of bank risk by other banks in the eurozone, with especially high domestic concentration in periphery countries. Finally, column 4 presents the estimates for the role of bonds issued by systemically important banks. It shows that the largest holding effects accrue to domestic bonds issued by banks with high SRISK, consistent with an increase in systemic risk. These results are important to show the consistency between the pledging results and the holdings results, as well as that the LOLR policy affects bank security portfolios, with relevant implications for risk allocation in the banking system.

We also examine whether the pledging and holding effects are consistent not only qualitatively but also quantitatively. To this end, we collapse the more granular pledging data to the same level of aggregation as the holdings data (ie, from ISIN-bank-month to ISIN-country-quarter). We present the main result of the paper—domestic versus foreign margin—for pledging in column 5 and compare it with the estimates for holdings in column 2 ofTable 6. We find that the two margins are aligned also in terms of magnitudes. A one-standard-deviation increase in the haircut gap is associated with a 2.4% increase in holding and 2.3% increase in pledging of domestic bank bonds compared to the mean value held/pledged as a share of the value outstanding at the country level.40

To summarize, by showing that banks increase by more holding and pledging of collateral issued by other similar banks, our results support the idea that the decision of banks to purchase securities issued by their peers is reinforced by their monitoring abilities (see, e.g.,Rochet and Tirole 1996;Freixas, Parigi, and Rochet 2000). This is also consistent with the banking industry being very opaque (Morgan 2002) and hence with more substantial monitoring needs. Furthermore, we do not find evidence that banks located in periphery countries increase their holding and pledging of bonds issued by banks located in foreign countries. This suggests that our findings cannot be explained by the diversification or insurance motive as inAllen and Gale (2000,2007) or by the broad reach for yield (see, e.g.,Adrian and Shin 2010). According to the latter, we should expect a higher loading on risky, high-haircut-gap securities across the entire set of high yield assets regardless of the country of issuance (ie, domestic or foreign bonds). In contrast, we find evidence of increased holding and pledging only of domestic securities issued by banks in periphery countries. Moreover, the findings show that banks purchase and pledge more bonds issued by domestic systemically important banks in line with systemic bailout theories (Acharya and Yorulmazer 2007;Farhi and Tirole 2012).

article.6 Bank Bonds Issuance:
So far, we have shown that in response to higher haircut gaps, banks increase their demand for bank-issued bonds. In this section we explore the supply side response to LOLR funding in the bank bond market. In other words, do higher haircut gaps also affect the decision to issue new securities? We investigate the issuance response in two ways. First, we start with a security level analysis where we focus on the events of new bank bond issuances. We estimate the following equation: α α α β γ ϵ y s , t = α t + α r + α c + β E t − 1 ( HaircutGap s , t ) + γ X s , t + ϵ s , t , (9) where the outcome variable denotes (1) the log value of security s issued at time t , (2) the spread, or (3) the log value of bond maturity. 41 We analyze all three characteristics of bonds issued to provide a comprehensive assessment of the role of security demand factors in affecting the decision of banks to issue new bonds. We regress the outcome variable on the haircut gap measure of the security. Because the security is issued only at time t , previous levels of the haircut gap cannot be observed. We address this issue by constructing an expected value of the haircut gap. To compute the ECB haircut, we rely on publicly available ECB haircut policy rules. We employ the machine learning algorithm to predict the private market haircut using the issuer rating as of t— 1 along with the actual maturity, asset type and coupon type of the security s (for details, see Internet Appendix A ). We saturate the specification with control variables and fixed effects (time, rating, and country of issuer). 42

Table 8reports the results. Panel A, columns 1 and 2, shows that banks with ex ante higher haircut gaps issue more bonds. As a next step, we examine the effect on bond spreads and maturities. The results show that higher haircut gaps are associated not only with the larger quantity of issuance of new bonds by banks but also with lower spreads (columns 3 and 4) and longer maturity (columns 5 and 6). These estimates are also economically significant. Specifically, a one-standard-deviation increase in haircut gap is associated with a 15.9% increase in the value of bank bonds issued, 50 bp lower spread and 43% longer maturity. Taken together, these results (higher volume and lower spread) are consistent with a higher demand for high haircut gap bank bonds from other banks that can use them to pledge at the ECB.43

(10)

HaircutGapb,t−1is the bank-level haircut gap constructed as average security level haircut gap weighted by the value outstanding. In this case, as there are (existing) observed haircut gaps for other securities outstanding of bankb, we do not need to predict the bank-level haircut gap. Table 8, panel B, reports the estimates for the bank-level analysis. Positive and statistically significant coefficients in panel B suggest that the issuance of new debt goes beyond the replacement of maturating debt. Higher haircut gaps incentivize banks to issue additional bank bonds and increase the total dependence on bond market financing. A one-standard-deviation increase in haircut gap is associated with a 7.5% to 10.7% increase in the amount of bank debt outstanding.

article.7 Additional Systemic Risk Implications:
7.1 Balance sheet analysis of issuing and pledging banks

What are the balance sheet implications of the haircut gap channel for banks? In this section, we examine how bond issuing banks and pledging/holding banks adjust their balance sheets in response to changes in haircut gaps. Specifically, we ask whether increased holdings of bank bonds within the banking system are offset by other types of interbank linkages, mainly through interbank loans, which make up another sizeable asset class in bank interconnectedness. This analysis allows us to understand whether the haircut gap channel leads to an increase in bank interconnectedness through larger cross-bond holdings or just a substitution between bonds and interbank loans with no effect on bank interlinkages on net. Using individual bank balance sheet data,Table 9reports the balance sheet responses for both bond issuing banks and pledging banks in panels A and B, respectively.
