We start by focusing on the issuing banks in panel A. First, column 1 confirms the results from the issuance analysis (Section 6) that higher haircut gaps are associated with higher amount of bank bond issuances. Importantly, we do not find any effect on interbank loans or other wholesale loans that banks receive (column 2). Furthermore, we also do not find any substitution with deposits (column 3). As a result, from the perspective of an issuing bank, there is no substitution with other forms of funding. Consequently, issuing bank balance sheet expands (column 4) and the leverage (debt-to-equity ratio) rises (column 5).

In terms of economic magnitudes, the coefficient estimates in panel A ofTable 9suggest that for an average issuing bank, a one-standard-deviation increase in the haircut gap is associated with a 13% or EUR 2.87bn increase in bonds issued (column 1) as well as a 2% or EUR 2.75bn increase in total liabilities (column 4). In addition, this is associated with a 2% increase in the debt-to-equity ratio (column 5). Overall, the economic magnitudes provide additional evidence in support of a substantial expansion of bank balance sheets and the lack of substitution with other forms of funding on the liability side.

Second, in panel B, we report the effect on pledging banks. In column 1, we show that banks that pledge more high haircut gap bonds increase their dependence on the ECB liquidity. In column 2, we examine the effect of pledging/holding of high haircut gap bonds on the size of bank bond portfolio. Specifically, a one-standard-deviation increase in the haircut gap is associated with a 19%,or EUR 1.02bn, increase in ECB liquidity (column 1) and a 13%, or EUR 1.35bn, increase in bank bonds held (column 2).44The estimates suggest that banks with more ex ante high haircut gap bonds pledged are also more likely to increase their bond holding portfolio. This is an additional piece of evidence in support of a rise in bank interconnectedness via bank bond cross-holdings that we document throughout the paper. Importantly, we do not find any effect on lending to other banks (the coefficient in column 3 is not statistically significant).45

To summarize, we do not find any substitution to/from alternative forms of financing. A higher volume of bank bonds among banks implies a higher connection between pair of banks, as bank bonds donotsubstitute for other type of bank-to-bank linkages, notably interbank loans, which are very high in Europe (on 15% average of total bank liabilities). Consistent results for both issuing banks (expansion in bond financing on the liabilities side without substitution away from other types of borrowing from banks) and holding banks (expansion of bank bond holdings on the asset side without substitution away from other types of lending to banks) suggests that the haircut gap channel of the LOLR led to higher interconnectedness and systemic risk in the banking system.

7.2 Haircut gap and correlation in market outcomes between banks

Finally, we examine the impact of the haircut gap on market-based measures of systemic risk. Specifically, we focus on the ex post correlation of bond prices between pledging and issuing banks. In the baseline, we focus on bond prices because stock prices and CDS spreads are only available for large, publicly listed banks, while bond prices offer significantly larger coverage across banks in the euro area. We examine the effect on stock prices as well as CDS spreads in the Internet Appendix . We test the hypothesis whether an increase in holdings of other banks’ bonds for pledging purposes triggered by higher haircut gaps subsequently increases the correlation of banks’ market outcomes. Formally, we estimate the following regression: α α β β δ ϵ Corr ( Bond Prices i , b , t ) = α t + α i , b + β 1 HaircutGap i , t − 1 + + β 2 ( HaircutGap i , t − 1 × H i , b ) + δ X i , b , t − 1 + ϵ i , b , t . (11)

The empirical strategy builds on our baseline pledging analysis. We introduce a few modifications that are relevant for this exercise. We perform this analysis at the pledging bank—issuing bank (pair) level by aggregating all bank bonds of a bank into a single bond price index.46The outcome variable is the correlation of bond price indexes of the issuing bankiand the pledging bankbcomputed based on data between periodtandt+ 24 months. As a result, the outcome variable is an ex post measure of bond price correlation. For the purpose of this analysis, we collapse the haircut gap measure at the issuing bank level as a weighted average of the haircut gaps of securities issued by banki.47In addition to the time fixed effects (αt), we use pledging bank—issuing bank (pair) fixed effects (⁠ααi,b⁠) to control for any time-invariant heterogeneity at the bank-pair level, such as average correlation of their bond prices and nonrandom matching between banks. We also control for key observable characteristics, such as lagged correlation between bond prices of banks and correlations between prices of bank bonds and domestic sovereign bonds to account for the fact that in the midst of a crisis, banking sector and sovereign risk are strongly intertwined.48Finally, we report additional results also with country-time fixed effects that allow us to further account for time-varying observable and unobservable heterogeneity at the country-time level, such as sovereign-banks nexus risk or sovereign debt sustainability issues.

Table 10presents the results. Consistent with the pledging and holding analysis, in columns 1 and 2, we show that higher haircut gaps are associated with higher ex post correlation of bond prices between domestic banks (the bank that pledges the security and the bank that issues the security). This result suggests that increased cross-holdings of domestic bank bonds contributed to an increase in systemic risk ex post as measured by an increased correlation between banks in market outcomes.

InTable 11, we further show that a high haircut gap is associated with a high correlation of bond prices especially in periods of high sovereign stress. We expand the specification described inEquation (11)to focus on the interaction of haircut gap and the yield of the 10-year sovereign bond issued by the domestic government of the issuing bank. We define the variable Sovereign Yieldc(b),t−1as a dummy that takes the value of one if the sovereign bond yield ranks in the top 20% of all sovereign yields across time, and zero otherwise. A positive and statistically significant interaction coefficient reported in column 1 suggests that a high haircut gap is associated with a high correlation of bank bond prices, especially in periods of high sovereign stress represented by high government yields, which is consistent with the presence of “doom loop” style spiral effects. This finding is robust to the inclusion of additional control variables in columns 2 and 3.50In columns 4–6, we present the effects when replacing the haircut gap with the fitted values of bond pledging, and in columns 7–9, we report the results using a continuous measure of sovereign yields. All of the results are consistent with the baseline and highlight that the interbank holdings of bonds led to an accentuation of the “doom loop” at times of domestic sovereign stress.

Taken together, our findings suggest that a rise in interconnectedness between banks triggered by LOLR policy played out to the disadvantage of banks as it led to stronger comovement in market outcomes between banks, especially with larger drops in bond prices during bad times. Our results therefore uncover an important role of bank bonds as a source of collateral for other banks and our results suggest that LOLR policy increases systemic risk at the margin by increasing bank cross-linkages.
article.8 Conclusion:
This paper examines whether and how LOLR policy affects bank interconnectedness, thereby affecting systemic risk. Our analysis uses a novel micro-level data set that links the information on held and pledged securities with the haircuts applied by the LOLR and the private repo markets. We investigate the haircut gap channel of the LOLR, that is, the difference between the haircut applied by the private market and the central bank for securities that banks pledge as collateral in repo operations. For identification, our analysis focuses on the provision of liquidity by the ECB in the period 2009–2015. The eurozone offers an ideal setting to study the implications of LOLR policy for three reasons:(1)banks are at the core of the financial system in Europe;(2)during the Global Financial Crisis and the European Sovereign Debt Crisis, the ECB expanded its liquidity provision to an unprecedented scale and scope; and(3)the extraordinary availability of ECB and private market data allows us to properly analyze the haircut gap channel of LOLR.

We find that LOLR funding incentivizes banks to increase holding and pledging of bonds associated with higher haircut gaps. This effect is more pronounced for banks with lower liquidity ratios and riskier banks with lower credit ratings, consistent with liquidity constraints and expected subsidy maximization motives for seeking central bank liquidity. Higher haircut gaps only incentivize banks (with LOLR access), not other sectors (without LOLR access), to increase bond holdings.

We find that the effect of haircut gap is stronger for bonds issued by other banks than by sovereigns. In particular, banks increase the holding and pledging of bonds issued by other interconnected banks. We show that interconnectedness is stronger across similar, domestic banks. Within domestic banks, high haircut gaps further increase the holding and pledging of bonds issued by systemically important banks.

Consistent with an increase in the demand for bank bonds with higher haircut gaps, we also document that LOLR policy stimulates issuance of these bonds by banks, at lower spreads and longer term maturities. Moreover, banks with higher haircut gaps increase their overall dependence on bond market financing and do not substitute with other bank-to-bank linkages, such as the interbank loans, thereby leading to an increase in total liabilities and in leverage. Finally, we show that in response to the increased cross-holdings of bank bonds, banks subsequently become more correlated in market outcomes. We find that higher haircut gaps are associated with higher ex post correlation of market outcomes between pledging and issuing banks, especially during periods of low-market returns, and for systemically important issuing banks.

All in all, our results uncover an important role for bank bonds as a source of collateral for other banks and show that LOLR policy increases bank interconnectedness, by encouraging the cross-holding of bank debt. Taken together, we show a new haircut channel of monetary operations, that encourages an increase in the concentration of bank risk within the banking sector. This potentially adverse effect of LOLR policies should ultimately be evaluated against other positive effects, including the beneficial effects of LOLR operations in terms of absorbing illiquidity risk and supporting bank credit to the private sector previously identified in the literature and the positive effect on the issuance of bank bonds uncovered in this paper.
